attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of epigastric pain and loose, foul-smelling stools. The pain is constant, though fluctuating in severity. He has a history of heavy alcohol consumption with multiple previous episodes of acute pancreatitis but stopped drinking six months ago. He takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears thin with a BMI of 19 kg/m2. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 118/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness but no masses. A lipase level is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation to establish the underlying diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of chronic pancreatitis, a progressive inflammatory condition characterised by irreversible damage to the pancreas. The classic triad includes chronic abdominal pain, maldigestion (steatorrhoea), and weight loss. Chronic pancreatitis typically develops after repeated episodes of acute pancreatitis, with alcohol being the most common cause in developed countries.

Computed tomography of the abdomen is the most appropriate initial investigation as it can demonstrate characteristic features such as pancreatic calcifications, ductal dilatation, and parenchymal atrophy. CT also helps exclude other conditions and can identify complications such as pseudocysts or malignancy.

Gastroscopy and colonoscopy would not be the first-line investigation as they do not directly visualise the pancreas. While they may be useful to exclude other gastrointestinal disease, the history strongly suggests pancreatic pathology.

Abdominal ultrasound has limited utility in pancreatic imaging due to overlying bowel gas and body habitus. While it may show dilated ducts or calcifications, CT provides much better pancreatic visualization.

Faecal calprotectin is primarily used to detect intestinal inflammation in conditions like inflammatory bowel disease and would not be helpful in diagnosing chronic pancreatitis.

Faecal elastase measurement is useful for diagnosing pancreatic exocrine insufficiency but does not directly diagnose chronic pancreatitis. It would be more appropriate as a subsequent test to assess for complications of chronic pancreatitis.

Complications of chronic pancreatitis include pancreatic exocrine insufficiency leading to malabsorption, diabetes mellitus (type 3c) due to destruction of islet cells, chronic pain, pseudocyst formation, and an increased risk of pancreatic cancer. Management involves addressing the underlying cause (e.g., alcohol cessation), pain control, pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy, and nutritional support.
learningPoints: CT imaging is the first-line investigation for diagnosing chronic pancreatitis, showing calcifications and ductal changes.
Faecal elastase is useful for diagnosing pancreatic exocrine insufficiency in chronic pancreatitis.
Chronic pancreatitis complications include malabsorption, diabetes, and increased risk of pancreatic cancer.
references: Chronic pancreatitis: Negotiating the complexities of diagnosis and management. Australian Journal for General Practitioners. 2015 Sep 21;44:718–22. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2015/october/chronic-pancreatitis-negotiating-the-complexities

Gardner TB, Adler DG, Forsmark CE, Sauer BG, Taylor JR, Whitcomb DC. ACG Clinical Guideline: Chronic Pancreatitis. Official journal of the American College of Gastroenterology | ACG [Internet]. 2020;115(3). Available from: https://journals.lww.com/ajg/fulltext/2020/03000/acg_clinical_guideline__chronic_pancreatitis.9.aspx
